coredata.dc:title: Development of ternary nanocomposite based on ferrocenyl-modified graphene quantum dots for high-performance energy storage applications
coredata.dc:creator: Payami, Elmira
Teimuri-Mofrad, Reza
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots were synthesized in a simple one-step procedure using citric acid as starting material. 4-Ferrocenylbutanol as a battery-type electrode material was further used to modify the surface of graphene quantum dots based on hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions. Finally, the nanocomposites with different compositions were synthesized using different amounts of CNT and Fe3O4. Their electrochemical performance shows the properties of the final nanocomposite for practical application in supercapacitors. The final nanocomposite exhibits ideal battery-type capacity, including high capacity of 258 mAh g−1 at 2.5 A g−1, excellent cycle life (89.2 %), and high energy and power density of 75 Wh kg−1 and 6000 W kg−1 (in two electrode system), respectively. The results show superior electrochemical performance and improved ion transfer rate between the electrode/electrolyte surface. The superiority of the final nanocomposite indicates its special properties for potential applications in energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ferrocene
Graphene quantum dots
Nanocomposite
Battery-type supercapacitor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CNT-Fe3O4 nanohybrid and Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4 nanocomposites
Electrochemical investigation of the as-prepared samples
Electrochemical performances of Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) symmetric cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project is supported by the research grant of the University of Tabriz (number SAD/4786-1402/01/14 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FT-IR spectra of the all as-prepared samples.
FE-SEM images of (a) Qdots-Fc, (b) CNT-Fe3O4, (c) Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(10 %), (d) Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %), and (e) Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(30 %).
FE-SEM images of (a) Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(10 %), (b) Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %), and (c) Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(30 %) at low-magnification.
The XRD patterns of the all as-prepared samples.
XPS (a) survey, (b) C1s, (c) O1s, and (d) Fe2p spectra of Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %).
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of (a) Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(10 %), (b) Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %), (c) Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(30 %) and (d) BJH pore size distribution plots.

(a) CV curves of CNT, Fe3O4 and CNT-Fe3O4 nanohybrids at 50 mV s−1, (b) The relationship between specific capacity and mass fraction of Fe3O4 in CNT-Fe3O4 nanohybrids, (c) CV curves of all synthesized samples at 50 mV s−1, (d) CV curves of the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) nanocomposite at various scan rates, (e) The linear graph of anodic and cathodic peak current as a function of the square root of the scan rate, and (f) The linear curve of logarithm of peak current as a function of the logarithm of scan rate.

(a) GCD curves of all synthesized samples at 2.5 A g−1, (b) GCD curve of Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) nanocomposite at various current densities, (c) Specific capacities versus current density, and (d) Charge storage retention and CE of the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) nanocomposite.
The performance mechanism of Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4 electrode material.
(a) Operating voltage, (b) CV curves, (c) GCD curves, (d) Ragone plot, and (e) Charge storage retention of fabricated symmetric cell.
(a) Schematic diagram of a hybrid supercapacitor and (b) Exact electron transfer in the final nanocomposite.
Synthesis route of the Qdots-Fc nanohybrid.
The specific capacity values of electrode materials at different current densities.
Comparison among this project with some reported works.

head.ce:title: Development of ternary nanocomposite based on ferrocenyl-modified graphene quantum dots for high-performance energy storage applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots were synthesized in a simple one-step procedure using citric acid as starting material. 4-Ferrocenylbutanol as a battery-type electrode material was further used to modify the surface of graphene quantum dots based on hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions. Finally, the nanocomposites with different compositions were synthesized using different amounts of CNT and Fe3O4. Their electrochemical performance shows the properties of the final nanocomposite for practical application in supercapacitors. The final nanocomposite exhibits ideal battery-type capacity, including high capacity of 258 mAh g−1 at 2.5 A g−1, excellent cycle life (89.2 %), and high energy and power density of 75 Wh kg−1 and 6000 W kg−1 (in two electrode system), respectively. The results show superior electrochemical performance and improved ion transfer rate between the electrode/electrolyte surface. The superiority of the final nanocomposite indicates its special properties for potential applications in energy storage devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, supercapacitors (SCs) have attracted much attention from researchers worldwide due to their amazing properties, which include high power density, superior rate capability, and high cycling stability [1,2]. On this topic, various carbon-based materials have been used as electrode materials with electrical double layer capacitance (EDLC), transition metal oxides, redox-active organic compounds, and conductive polymers as battery-type or pseudocapacitive materials [3–5].

Carbonaceous compounds are widely used for energy storage because of their excellent conductivity, large surface area, and superior chemical stability. Despite their outstanding properties, carbon-based materials suffer from low specific capacitance. However, the design and synthesis of novel carbon-based electrode materials with improved supercapacitor performance is being developed by different approaches, including pore engineering, heteroatom doping, and surface modification with suitable redox-active organic compounds [6,7].

Graphene quantum dots (Qdots) are one of the carbon-based materials widely used in energy storage and optical devices. Commonly, Qdots are prepared through hydrothermal processes using various materials such as carbohydrates, biomasses, citric acid, etc. As previously mentioned, one way to improve specific capacitance of carbon-based materials is to functionalize them with electroactive materials like ferrocene (Fc) [8–12].

Metallocenes are a particular class of organometallic complexes. A transition metal in its oxidation II state is associated with two cyclopentadienyl rings, which models the complex into a sandwich structure. Fc and its derivatives are studied as the most important organometallic compounds due to various outstanding properties such as excellent electrochemical properties, high thermal stability, low cost, etc., and are used in different applications like supercapacitors, sensors, solar cells, and catalysis. The excellent electrochemical properties of Fc derivatives, in which Fe acts as the electron donor segment and cyclopentadiene as the electron acceptor segment, can give battery-type properties to carbonaceous materials and thus improve the supercapacitor performance of these electrode materials [13–28].

The surface modification of carbon nanotube (CNT) using different materials and the combination of the unique physiochemical properties of an individual CNT such as high conductivity and good mechanical and electro-optical behavior expands the applications of functionalized CNT [29,30]. Additionally, the combination of CNT with transition metal oxides, especially magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles, improves the specific capacitance, cycling stability, and conductivity of the final nanocomposite.

Some transition metal oxides such as Fe3O4, TiO2, Ni(OH)2, and Co3O4 are used as battery-type electrode materials for supercapacitors due to their high specific capacity [31–33]. Among them, Fe3O4 has attracted the considerable attention of researchers for its merits, which include inexpensive, has high specific capacity, earth-abundance, and is environmentally friendly. Despite these attractive properties, Fe3O4 suffers from low rate capability and poor cycling performance. To solve these problems, many efforts have been made to hybridize Fe3O4 with carbon-based materials by various methods [34–39]. For example, Zhang et al. [40] prepared a Fe3O4/graphene nanocomposite using a solvothermal method. The obtained hybrid supercapacitor exhibited high specific capacity and excellent supercapacitor performance. Li et al. [41] reported novel Fe3O4-doped carbon spheres. The resulting nanohybrid showed high energy density and power density of 45 Wh kg−1 and 400 W kg−1, respectively, and excellent cycling performance.

Based on the above content, the present work demonstrates preparation a novel ternary nanocomposite based on ferrocenyl-modified Qdots. For this purpose, we first modified the surface of the Qdots with 4-Ferrocenylbutanol as an Fc derivative to improve the supercapacitor performance of Qdots. Then, the final ternary nanocomposite was prepared by a simple physical mixing method. After a thorough characterization of the resulting electrode materials, the symmetrical device was designed and fabricated with the final nanocomposite. The novelty of this work is synthesizing ternary Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4 nanocomposites, which showed a notable electrochemical performance. The observed performance improvement is because of the synergic effects between nanocomposite components. The combination of Qdots and Fc dramatically improves the capabilities of electrode materials. The conductively embedded Fc, in turn, boosts the charge and ion accumulated on the electrodes through its reversible faradaic reactions. Moreover, CNT with high chemical stability and high conductivity could improve the poor electrical conductivity and low cycling stability of Fe3O4, which also helps to more effectually utilize the redox inherent of Fe3O4. Also, CNT-Fe3O4 can set up conductive networks along the inner matrix of nanocomposite, reaching highly effective conducting channels for electron and ions transportation.
All chemicals used in this project were purchased from Merck and Sigma-Aldrich and used as obtained.

Qdots were synthesized according to a previously described method [12]. Specifically, 1 g of citric acid (CA) was pyrolyzed at 200 °C in an oven. After 30 min, an orange liquid was obtained, indicating the preparation of Qdots. Then, 1 M NaOH was slowly added to the molten liquid at room temperature to neutralize it and dried in an oven (60 °C).

To a suspension of Qdots in DMF was added a solution of 4-ferrocenylbutanol [25] in DMF (10 mg ml−1) and then stirred for 4 days. After this time, the obtained suspension was centrifuged and washed with DMF until no orange color was present. The resulting precipitate was finally dried in a vacuum oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CNT-Fe3O4 nanohybrid and Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The CNT was dispersed in acetone for 4 h. Then four different suspensions of magnetite [25] in acetone (5 wt%, 10 wt%, 15 wt%, and 20 wt%) were added to the CNT suspension obtained above. CNT-Fe3O4(5 %), CNT-Fe3O4(10 %), CNT-Fe3O4(15 %), and CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) were obtained after evaporating the solvent and drying in an oven.

After electrochemical evaluation of the nanohybrids, the CNT-Fe3O4(10 %) with 10 wt% Fe3O4 showed better specific capacity (see Fig. 6a and b) and was then used to prepare the final nanocomposites. The final nanocomposites were prepared using the same method as described above with different amounts of CNT-Fe3O4(10 %) nanohybrid. The resulting nanocomposites were named as Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(10 %), Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) and Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(30 %) for the 10 wt%, 20 wt% and 30 wt% of CNT-Fe3O4(10 %) nanohybrid, respectively.

The microstructure of the as-synthesized electrode materials was determined using the FT-IR technique with a Bruker Tensor 270 spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Bruker AXS-D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu Ka radiation (1.5418 Å)) was used to characterize the phase composition. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis (MIRA3 TESCAN) was then performed to determine the proportion of each component in the materials. The surface morphology of the as-prepared nanomaterials was investigated using field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) (MIRA3 TESCAN).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical investigation of the as-prepared samples
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical experiments of all the as-prepared materials were performed with a three-electrode system using Ag/AgCl, conductive graphite, and platinum plate as reference, working, and counter electrodes, respectively. A 1 M H2SO4 was used as the supporting electrolyte for the evaluation of cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The working electrode was generally prepared by coating the active electrode materials (85 wt%), acetylene black (10 wt%) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) (5 wt%) on conductive graphite. The CV plots were obtained at various scan rates from 10 to 150 mV s−1 and the GCD curves were measured at various current densities from 2.5 to 10 A g−1 in the voltage range from −0.2 to 0.9 V. The EIS test was performed at open circuit voltage (in a frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz).

After successful synthesizing of Qdots-Fc nanohybrid, different suspension of CNT-Fe3O4 in acetone was added to a suspension of Qdots-Fc to form Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4 nanocomposites (Scheme 2 ). The final nanocomposites were obtained after solvent evaporation.

As can be seen in Fig. 2 , the FT-IR spectrum exhibited the presence of various absorption peaks indicative of different functional groups on the surface of the Qdots, including the stretching vibration of OH (3420 cm−1), CH (2925 cm−1), CO (1700 cm−1), and CC (1600 cm−1) [42]. The appearance of new characteristic absorption peaks at 2965 cm−1 (CH), 1425 cm−1, 1155 cm−1 (C=C) and 460 cm−1 (Cp-Fe) in the FT-IR spectrum of Qdots-Fc confirms the modification of the Qdots surface with Fc-based compound [43]. As shown in Fig. 2, the FT-IR spectrum of CNT-Fe3O4 shows an adsorption peak at 558 cm−1, corresponding to the FeO stretching vibration and indicates the existence of magnetite nanoparticles in the CNT-Fe3O4 sample [44]. Moreover, the adsorption peaks at 3438 cm−1, 1721 cm−1, 1631 cm−1, and 1071 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching and bending vibrations of OH, CO, CC, and CO of the CNT functional groups, respectively [45]. Characteristic spectral peaks corresponding to each component are observed in the FT-IR spectra of Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(10 %), Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) and Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(30 %).

The typical FE-SEM technique was used to evaluate the surface morphology of as-synthesized electrode materials and the obtained micrographs are shown in Fig. 3 . The FE-SEM micrograph of the Qdots-Fc (Fig. 3a) shows a significant change that can be attributed to the Fc modification of the Qdots. According to our previous data [12], it is clear that the size growth of Qdots-Fc is due to the aggregation of Qdots after surface modification with Fc. This is most likely due to the hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking between the Fc moiety and the Qdots. As shown in Fig. 3b, the spherical Fe3O4 nanoparticles were distributed on the larva-like CNT surface, confirming the combination of CNTs and Fe3O4. FE-SEM micrographs (Fig. 3c–e) also show that the as-prepared nanocomposites containing different amounts of CNT-Fe3O4 have both Qdots-Fc and CNT-Fe3O4 components. In particular, Fig. 3 shows clearly visible Qdots due to lower amount of CNT-Fe3O4(10 %) in the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(10 %) nanocomposite. In contrast to Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(10 %), the CNTs are clearly visible in Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(30 %) (Fig. 3e). The Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) nanocomposite with a reasonable amount of CNT-Fe3O4(10 %) exhibits a uniform distribution of nanocomposite components. The formation of a uniform mixture of Qdots-Fc and CNT-Fe3O4(10 %) is due to the synergistic mixing of Qdots-Fc and CNT-Fe3O4(10 %), which results in less aggregation. This phenomenon enables ion transfer into the electrode materials and reduces the ion transport barrier, which suggests their potential applications in energy fields.

Also, the low-magnification FE-SEM micrographs of all the three final nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 4 . The low-magnification images of the nanocomposites (Fig. 4) show uniform CNT-Fe3O4 distribution in the Qdots-Fc. CNT-Fe3O4 are seen to be nicely attached to the Qdots-Fc via strong interactions including hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking, which leads to the increased size of aggregation of Qdots-Fc after surface modification with CNT-Fe3O4.

To further study the inner morphology, TEM micrograph of Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) nanocomposite was taken as depicted in Fig. 5 . The size growth of modified Qdots with Fc moiety via hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking is probably assigned to the aggregation phenomenon. The TEM image exhibits Fe3O4 nanoparticles uniformly dispersed on the surface of tube-like structures of CNT. It is worth noting that the CNT-Fe3O4 nanohybrid are strongly attached on the surface of modified Qdots (Qdots-Fc), indicating the strong interaction between nanocomposite components including electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking.

The EDX data approved existence of C element assigned to the Qdots, CNT, and Fc-based compound, O element attributed to the functional groups on the surface of Qdots, CNT and Fe3O4 nanoparticles and the OH functional group presented on the side chain of 4-ferrocenylbutanol, also, Fe element related to the Fc ring and Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Also, uniform distribution of the C, O, and Fe elements discovered by using the EDX technique over the surface of the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) (Fig. 6 ).

The XRD pattern of the Qdots (Fig. 7 ) shows a single broad peak at 2θ ∼ 25° (002), which corresponds to the graphitic structure of the Qdots without any contamination [46]. After surface modification of Qdots with Fc-based compounds, some new peaks appeared at 2θ = 16° and 19° in the XRD pattern of the Qdots-Fc nanohybrids, which are related to the (110) and (111) crystal planes, respectively [47]. These peaks indicate the successful modification of the Qdots surface with Fc-based compound. As shown in Fig. 7, the XRD pattern of CNT shows a strong and sharp peak at 2θ = 26°, which can be associated with the (002) reflection plane [48]. In the XRD pattern of Fe3O4, the obvious peaks appear at 18.4°, 30.4°, 35.5°, 37.1°, 43.1°, 53.7°, 57.1°, 62.7°, 71.5°, and 74.4°, which are assigned to planes (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440), (620), and (533), respectively [49]. The observed sharp peaks of the magnetite XRD pattern indicate the pure structure of the synthesized nanoparticles. The XRD pattern of CNT-Fe3O4(10 %) shows both CNT and Fe3O4 peaks, but the intensity of the diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 is low compared to the CNT peaks. The XRD curve of the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) nanocomposite shows all the characteristic peaks of all the components described above.

The chemical properties such as composition and bonding nature of final nanocomposite are investigated by XPS analysis. The survey XPS spectrum for Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) shows C1s and O1s at 285.1 eV and 533.2 eV, as depicted in Fig. 8a. Additionally, the peaks centered at 710 eV and 726 eV assigned to the Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 states (Fig. 8a), corresponded to the Fe3O4 and Fc ring of nanocomposite. Moreover, In the Fe 2p fine spectrum of the final nanocomposite, the Fe 2p region was deconvoluted into four peaks, as depicted in Fig. 8d. There were two spectral peaks centered at 732.1 eV, 714.9 eV, which were assigned to 2p1/2, 2p3/2 of Fe3+ species, respectively. And the other two bands at 725.2 eV and 710.5 eV could be corresponded to 2p1/2, 2p3/2 of Fe2+ species. The C1s peak can be deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 8b), which assigns to the C-C/CC, CO and CO and the O1s peaks can be deconvoluted into CO, C-O-C/C-OH and O=C-OH (Fig. 8c). These findings clearly prove the successful preparation of Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) nanocomposite.

Fig. 9 reveals the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(10 %), Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %), and Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(30 %). The synthesized nanocomposites exhibit a typical type IV isotherm with a hysteresis loop in the 0.8 > P/P0 > 0.4, obviously implying that as-synthesized nanomaterials have abundant mesoporosity. In addition, the slight rise of nitrogen adsorption at 0.9 < P/P0 < 1 indicates that a small proportion of macropores also presence in the nanomaterials. The pore size distribution of the as-prepared electrode materials exhibited a small range from 1 to 8 nm (Fig. 9d). This plentiful microporous inherent was significant for shortening the ion penetration pathways and fast ions transportation, enhancing the rate capability of the nanomaterials, thus resulting in an excellent capacitive performance. The specific surface area of the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(10 %), Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %), and Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(30 %) is found to be 124.6 m2 g−1, 168.3 m2 g−1, and 113.7 m2 g−1. As depicted in Fig. 9c, the specific surface area decreases with further increasing of the CNT-Fe3O4 content in the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(30 %). This is caused by CNT-Fe3O4 aggregates in the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(30 %), decreasing the exposed surface area.

The electrochemical performances of all as-prepared electrode materials were investigated using the CV technique. The specific capacity (mAh g−1) of the electrode materials can be calculated from the CV plots based on Eq. (1):
(1)
C s
=
1
3.6 mv
∫ Va Vc
I
V
dV
where I is the oxidation/reduction current, m is the mass of the active materials, and v the scan rate.

To achieve a better understanding of the electrochemical properties of the as-prepared nanohybrids, we first evaluated and compared the CV response of the different CNT-Fe3O4 nanohybrids at 50 mV s−1. As can be seen in Fig. 10a, a significant increase in redox peak current is observed with increasing Fe3O4 content up to 10 wt% and drops at 20 wt%. This result could indicate that a reasonable amount of Fe3O4 increases specific capacity of the nanohybrids, but further addition of Fe3O4 into the nanohybrids decreases the capacity due to increased resistance and slow ion diffusion within the electrode. Fig. 10b shows the relationship between the specific capacity and the mass ratio of Fe3O4 in the as-prepared nanohybrids. The specific capacity increases when the mass ratio of Fe3O4 increases up to 10 %, and it decreases with the further addition of Fe3O4 in the nanohybrids. This finding clearly suggests that the specific capacity of the as-prepared nanohybrids is not only due to EDLC, but also due to the battery-type inherent of Fe3O4, which is almost related to the Faradaic reaction of Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

Fig. 10c shows the CV curves of Qdots, Qdots-Fc, CNT-Fe3O4(10 %), Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(10 %), Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %), and Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(30 %) nanocomposites at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The CV response of pure Qdots at this scan rate has a smaller CV area than other electrode materials, which indicates lower values of specific capacitance, since the specific capacitance is proportional to the average area of a CV curve, as indicated in the above equation. The CV area in Fig. 10c also shows that the Qdots-Fc nanohybrid has the higher CV integration area compared to the pure Qdots, indicating the best capacity performances of the Qdots-Fc due to the presence of Fc as a battery-type electrode material. The shape of the CV curves for Qdots and Qdots-Fc shows that the curves are approximately rectangular and nonrectangular, respectively. The capacitive characteristics are mainly due to the EDLC and the battery type of the corresponding samples. The combination of the battery-type nature of Fe3O4 nanoparticles due to the redox peaks and their relationship with the reversible redox reaction in Fe(II) ↔ Fe(III) and the EDLC property of CNT improves the capacity of CNT-Fe3O4(10 %). The specific capacity first increases as the CNT-Fe3O4(10 %) content increases, and then starts to gradually decrease when the CNT-Fe3O4(10 %) content further increases, and their specific capacity values were182, 264, and 201 mAh g−1 for Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(10 %), Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %), and Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(30 %) nanocomposites, respectively, indicating that a reasonable amount of CNT-Fe3O4(10 %) can effectively increase the specific capacity of the nanocomposites, but the excess amount of CNT-Fe3O4(10 %) decreases the capacity value due to the high resistance and slow diffusion of ions within the active material.

The CV curves of Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) nanocomposite at different scan rates from 10 to 150 mV s−1 are shown in Fig. 10d. The nonrectangular and symmetric CV diagrams prove that both the EDLC (minor) of Qdots and CNTs and the faradaic battery-type nature (major) of Fc and Fe3O4 nanoparticles contribute. Moreover, it is evident that the CV shapes does not show much change at different sweep rates except for an increase in CV response, indicating excellent ion diffusion in the active materials.

A linear relationship for both anodic and cathodic peaks is obtained by the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) (Fig. 10e), indicating that redox behaviors of the nanocomposite controlled by a diffusion process. To further understand the significant electrochemical performance of the final nanocomposite, a study of peak current (i) associated with the sweep rate (v) was applied, based on the following equation:
(2)
i =
av b
where a and b re adjustable parameters (from 0.5 to 1). The b-value was given from the slope of the curve of log i versus log ʋ. Generally, b = 1 corresponds to a surface controlled process, whereas b = 0.5 indicates a diffusion-controlled process (battery-type behavior). As shown in Fig. 10f, the b-value is 0.62 for anodic peak and 0.60 for cathodic peak, which both values are close to 0.50, suggesting battery-type characteristics or typical diffusion-controlled kinetics.

The strong capacitive performance of all as-prepared electrode materials is further investigated using the GCD technique at a current density of 2.5 A g−1 (Fig. 11a). With the exception of Qdots, all GCD curves exhibit a typical nonlinear shape, indicative of a faradaic battery-type inherent. Moreover, all curves show high reversibility and very low IR drop during charge/discharge cycles. This is one of the good criteria for supercapacitors. As can be seen in Fig. 11a, the discharge time of Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) electrode is longer than that of the other electrode materials, which exhibit a high specific capacity that is in accordance with the CV results. Fig. 11b shows the GCD curves of the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) electrode at different current densities. As mentioned earlier, a clear slop change was observed in the discharge cycle, indicating that the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) exhibits a typical Faradaic battery-type inherent. This property is due to the redox reactions of the Fc ring and Fe3O4 nanoparticles on the surface of the electrode/electrolyte. The discharge time decreases with increasing current density, which is due to the limitation of ion transfer within the electrode/electrolyte surface during rapid discharge. At high current density, the ions do not have enough time to penetrate the surface of micropores. For this reason, the specific capacity at low current density is higher than at high current density.

The specific capacity of all electrode materials are calculated according to the GCD plots using the Eq. (3):
(3)
C s
=
I . Δ t
m
mAhg
− 1
where I is the current (mA), ∆t reveals the discharge time (h), and m exhibits the mass of the active materials (g).

Also, the specific capacity of electrode materials of C g−1 has been estimated by using the following relation:
(4)
C =
C s
× 3.6
Cg
− 1
where, C specific capacity in C g−1 and Cs specific capacity in mAh g−1.

The results show that 89.2 % of the charge storage capacity is still remained after 5000 GCD cycles (Fig. 11d). Coulomb efficiency (CE) is also an important factor, which indicates the stability of the final nanocomposite during the charge/discharge cycles. The CE of the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) sample is calculated using Eq. (5) [55].
(5)
η =
t d
t c
where t
c and t
d indicate the charging and discharging time, respectively. CE is close to ∼100 %, which clearly shows the superior cycling performance of the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) nanocomposite sample.

The results of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy are shown in Fig. 12 . The diameter of the semicircular portion, the intersection with the X-axis in the high-frequency region, and the slope of the straight line in the low-frequency region are attributed to the charge transfer resistance, the equivalent series resistance, and the diffusion resistance, respectively. The Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) electrode material exhibits lower equivalent series resistance than the other samples, indicating the excellent conductivity of the final nanocomposite. In addition, the low charge transfers resistance of Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) nanocomposite ensures rapid charge transfer at the electrode-electrolyte interface. Moreover, the straight line of Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) nanocomposite is sharper than that of the other nanocomposites in the low-frequency region, indicating enhanced ion diffusion.

Excellent electrochemical behavior of the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4 electrode material can be described by the different proofs as illustrated in Fig. 13 . Firstly, the Qdots as a carbon-based electrode material not only contribute to EDLC capacitance but also act as suitable support for decorating other nanocomposite components including Fc and CNT-Fe3O4. Secondly, the presence Fc derivative and Fe3O4 as faradaic battery-type materials provide more redox-active sites for interaction with ions, to give a highly conductive way for electron transfer among nanocomposite components. Also, the existence sufficient amount of CNT- Fe3O4 can supply more conductivity which can facilitate the electron or ion transfer across the nanocomposite components, and finally the modification of Qdots with Fc as a faradaic battery-type organometallic material not only increases the specific capacity but also facilitates ions and charge transport.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performances of Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) symmetric cells
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the potential application of the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) nanocomposite sample, a symmetrical cell was constructed by using Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) as both anode and cathode electrodes in 1 M H2SO4 as electrolyte. Fig. 14a shows the CV plots of the symmetric cell in different potential ranges at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1. It is clear that the stable potential window of the fabricated cell can be extended to 1.8 V without significant distortion of the CV plot. Fig. 14b shows the CV curves of the fabricated symmetric cell at various scan rates (10–100 mV s−1) without changing its shape, even at high scan rates, indicating very fast and effective ion transfer. Also, the GCD curves at different current densities (0.5–4 A g−1) are shown in Fig. 14c. The symmetric GCD shape proves a good capacitive property. The corresponding specific capacity was calculated based on the GCD curves, and the specific capacities as a function of different current densities are plotted in Fig. 14d. The constructed symmetric cell shows a specific capacity of 83.3, 71.3, 66.7, 58.3, and 48.1 mAh g−1 at a current density of 0.5, 1, 2, 3, and 4 Ag−1, respectively.

The Ragone diagram (energy-to-power relations) is an important factor for comparing different energy storage systems in terms of energy and power densities. The Ragone diagram of the fabricated cell calculated from the specific capacities and Eqs. (6) and (7) [56] shows that the highest energy and power densities are obtained at 75 Wh kg−1 and 6000 W kg−1, respectively. The calculated values exceed most other previously reported work (Fig. 14d) [57–60].
(6)
E =
1 2
CV
Wh
kg
‐ 1
(7)
P =
E
Δ t
W
kg
‐ 1
where E, P, C, V, and Δt are the energy density, power density, specific capacity, voltage window, and discharge time, respectively.

The cycling performance of our fabricated device was also investigated using the GCD test at a current density of 1 A g−1 and the corresponding curve is shown in Fig. 14e. It is clear that the fabricated cell shows superior cycling performance with a capacity retention of 91 % after 2500 GCD cycles. Thus, the fabricated cell is very promising for high performance supercapacitors.

In the fabricated symmetric cell, each electrode exhibits a hybrid charge storage mechanism (Fig. 15a). H+ reduction may occur at the cathode surface and rapidly adsorb onto the surface of carbon material. After hydrogen uptake, the anodic current was drastically increases, which is related to the electro-oxidation process of adsorbed hydrogen during the anodic scan. This is a clear reason for storing of hydrogen in the carbon electrode. The repeated hydrogen uptake provides an extra storage mechanism, which is happen in the cathode alongside the EDLC contribution, causing an increase in capacity. The existence of the redox peaks during the anodic and cathodic scans can be assigned to the redox-active electrode materials including Fc and Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The repetition these faradaic redox reactions on the surface of cathode allow us to achieve a notable enhance in capacity. The electron transport mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 15b. The stacked Qdots and CNT with sp2 hybridization supply an easy lateral pathway to the electrons transfer that might reduce the resistance of the electrode.

In summary, a ternary nanocomposite based on modified graphene quantum dots with Fc moiety, CNT and Fe3O4 with unique electrochemical properties was successfully prepared using a facile and simple physical mixing method. To optimize the synergistic effects between the components of the nanocomposite, the ratios of CNT to Fe3O4 and Qdots-Fc to CNT-Fe3O4 were properly adjusted in this project. Excellent electrochemical results are obtained by mixing 20 wt% CNT- Fe3O4(10 %) with ferrocenyl-modified Qdots. The final nanocomposite exhibits a high specific capacity of 258 mAh g−1 at 2.5 A g−1, which is almost nine times higher than that of pure Qdots (30 mAh g−1 at 2.5 A g−1). The electrode also exhibits 89.2 % charge retention after 5000 GCD cycles, indicating the good cycling stability of the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) electrode. Finally, the corresponding symmetric cell was fabricated using the Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) nanocomposite sample. Our fabricated symmetric cell demonstrates a high energy and power density 75 Wh kg−1 and 6000 W kg−1, as well as considerable cycling stability (91 % after 2500 cycles). The novel Qdots-Fc/CNT-Fe3O4(20 %) electrode material shows considerable potential for battery-type supercapacitors.
Elmira Payami: Investigation, Writing - Original draft preparation, Methodology, Software, Reza Teimuri-Mofrad: Supervision, Conceptualization, Validation, Writing - Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project is supported by the research grant of the University of Tabriz (number SAD/4786-1402/01/14).
